title: Spinning a crepe yarn
subtitle: How to construct a four-strand crepe yarn by counter-plying two energised two-ply yarns
excerpt: Crepe yarn is a four-strand construction built in three stages: spin singles with high twist, ply two singles into a deliberately over-twisted two-ply, then counter-ply two of those energised two-plies together. The result is a round, smooth, dense yarn with very low halo and high durability.
sourceNotes: Crepe yarn construction is a standard advanced spinning technique documented in Mabel Ross's 'The Essentials of Yarn Design' and taught in UK and US spinning guilds. Twist mechanics are consistent across all sources.

glossaryTerms.definition: A two-ply yarn spun with deliberately higher twist than a balanced two-ply, so it retains twist energy and coils when released. Energised plies are the structural building block of crepe yarn; their stored energy is locked in during the final counter-plying stage.

The final plying stage in crepe yarn construction, in which two energised two-ply yarns are plied together in the same direction as the original singles twist (opposite to the first ply direction). Counter-plying locks in the crepe structure and produces a round, tightly bound yarn.

The direction of spin or ply: Z-twist runs clockwise when looking at the forming yarn; S-twist runs anticlockwise. In crepe construction, tracking the twist direction at each stage is essential because each successive step deliberately alternates direction.

content.text: Crepe yarn is built in three stages, each one deliberately storing twist energy that the next stage locks in. The
alternates at each stage. Singles spin one way. The first ply runs in the opposite direction with high twist to produce an

two of those energised plies in the original singles direction produces the finished crepe. Resist the impulse to correct the over-twisted ply in stage two: it will look wrong and feel lively, and this is entirely intentional.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair, scarves, and cuffs away from the drive band and flyer. The flyer turns fast and catches fabric instantly.

items.name: Fibre for spinning: combed top or carded roving, 200 g
Spinning wheel with at least four bobbins
Lazy kate or two-bobbin holder for the counter-plying stage
Measuring tape for checking wraps per inch

content.text: Set the wheel to a ratio two to three points higher than you would normally use for the intended yarn weight. For a DK-weight crepe, a ratio of 10:1 to 12:1 gives approximately the right amount of singles twist. Spin four singles onto four bobbins, keeping them as consistent as possible. The singles will look lively and springy; when you release a length free it will kink back on itself. This is intentional. Divide your fibre equally by weight across all four singles so each bobbin carries the same length.

Take two bobbins and ply them together in the opposite direction to the singles at a higher ratio than you would use for a balanced two-ply: for DK weight, a 7:1 to 9:1 ratio. The resulting two-ply will be over-twisted: it coils and kinks when released and will not hang straight. This is correct. Wind the two energised plies onto two separate bobbins and do not allow them to ply back on themselves before stage three. Repeat with the remaining two singles bobbins to make a second energised two-ply.

Place both energised two-ply bobbins on a lazy kate. Ply them together in the same direction as the original singles, which is the opposite direction to stage two, at a ratio of 8:1 to 10:1 for DK weight. The two coiling energised plies lock into each other and produce a round, smooth yarn. Let a length hang free: a properly balanced crepe yarn hangs straight without coiling. If it still coils, add more counter-ply twist. The finished yarn will be noticeably denser and more tightly structured than a conventional two-ply at the same wraps-per-inch count.

Wind the crepe yarn onto the niddy-noddy into a skein, tie at four points, and soak in warm water for 20 minutes. Snap once firmly to set the twist and hang to dry with a weight. Crepe yarn changes very little on finishing compared with a conventional two-ply; the structure is already set by the counter-plying. The finished yarn has very low halo and a compact, smooth surface suited to colourwork and mosaic knitting.

items.problem: The finished yarn still kinks and coils after counter-plying
items.cause: The counter-plying stage used too low a ratio and did not insert enough twist to lock the energised plies together
items.fix: Unwind the counter-plied length and re-ply at a higher ratio. Crepe yarn needs more counter-ply twist than seems intuitive. Continue increasing counter-ply twist until a test length hangs straight.

items.problem: The finished crepe yarn feels much harder and wiry rather than dense and smooth
items.cause: The singles were over-twisted relative to the fibre and the intended yarn weight
items.fix: Reduce the singles ratio by one step for the next attempt. Use a longer-staple fibre if possible. Longer fibres tolerate higher twist without becoming wiry.
